7 min read
Just now

--

Resources
♟️ Play against the AI
💻 Source code

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most chess engines can already play at virtually any rating level.
Want to play against a 1000-rated opponent? That’s easy. Modern engines like Stockfish can simply reduce their search depth, limit computation, or intentionally choose weaker moves.
The result is an opponent that wins about as often as a 1000-rated player.
But there’s one problem.
It doesn’t actually play like a human.
The mistakes made by weakened chess engines often feel artificial. Beginners hang pieces because they miss tactics. Intermediate players overestimate attacks or underestimate positional weaknesses. Strong club players recognize tactical opportunities but sometimes misjudge long-term plans.
Weakened engines don’t naturally make any of those kinds of mistakes, they simply choose objectively worse moves.
That got me wondering:
Could an AI learn to play chess the same way humans do instead of simply pretending to be weaker?
Over the past few weeks I built a transformer model trained entirely on human chess games to answer that question.
I also built a web app where you can play against the models I trained yourself. If you’d like to see how my 1000- and 2000-rated AI actually feel, you can try test them out here. (Hosting on free tier so give the App a minute or so to come online)
The Idea
Instead of asking a model to find the best move, I asked it a different question:
“Given this board position, what move would a human player of a particular rating most likely choose?”
This shifts the problem completely from creating a perfect chess player, to creating a human chess player.
If the model succeeds, a 1000-rated model shouldn’t just have a 1000 Elo playing strength, it should make the same kinds of decisions, good and bad, that real 1000-rated players make.
Likewise, a model trained on stronger players should naturally develop more sophisticated strategic understanding without ever being explicitly programmed to do so.
Building the Dataset
The first challenge was obtaining enough human games.
Using the Chess.com public API, I collected player data for 10 000 players from each of ten countries with large chess communities (100 000 in total):

• United States

• India

• Brazil

• Indonesia

• Philippines

• Great Britain

• Canada

• Germany

• France

• Turkey
From those players I constructed two separate datasets:

• approximately 1000 Elo players

• approximately 2000 Elo players
To keep the data representative of current playing strength, I filtered players using several criteria:

• Rapid games only

• rating within ±100 Elo of the target

• at least 50 rated Rapid games

• rating deviation below 100

• games played within the previous two years
After filtering, I selected 500 players for each rating group.
Instead of training on complete games, every single position after every half-move became a training example.
Each sample contained:

• the complete board position

• side to move

• castling rights

• the move the human actually played
This expanded the datasets into approximately 10 million training positions for the 1000 Elo model and 14 million positions for the 2000 Elo model.
Teaching a Transformer to Play Chess
Every board position was converted into a sequence of tokens describing the entire game state.
Those tokens were fed into a four-layer transformer encoder that learned to predict the move a human actually selected.
Unlike traditional chess engines, the model never searched future positions.
Its only objective was simple:
Predict the move a human would make.
Interested in the implementation? The full source code, training pipeline, and model architecture are available on GitHub here.
How Well Did It Work?
Evaluating a model like this is surprisingly tricky.
In many chess positions there isn’t a single “correct” move. Several moves may all be perfectly reasonable choices for a human player.
Because of that I measured two metrics:

• Top-1 Accuracy: Did the model’s most likely move match the human move?

• Top-5 Accuracy: Was the human move among the model’s five most likely predictions?
The results surprised me.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Results of Model EvaluationAt first glance, correctly predicting the human move only 36% of the time may not seem particularly impressive. However, the average chess position offers roughly 35 legal moves. Rather than selecting one move out of dozens at random, the model identified the exact move chosen by the human player in the given position more than one-third of the time. Even more strikingly, the human move appeared among the model’s top five predictions nearly 75% of the time, indicating that it had learned to identify the same small set of plausible candidate moves that human players typically consider.
One result I found particularly interesting was that both models achieved almost identical accuracy despite being trained on players with vastly different skill levels. In many positions, a 1000-rated player and a 2000-rated player will choose completely different moves. The fact that each model could reliably predict the choices made by its own rating group suggests that it wasn’t simply learning “good chess”, it was learning the distinct habits, strengths, and mistakes that characterize players at different levels.
Do They Play Differently?
The evaluation metrics showed that both models could reliably predict human moves. But the real question was whether they would actually feel different to play against.
The 1000 Elo model behaved exactly how I’d hoped. In the opening below, it left established opening theory after only two moves. Rather than rapidly developing its pieces and fighting for control of the center, it cautiously advanced pawns one square at a time, as though it were hesitant to commit its pieces. It immediately reminded me of the kinds of openings beginners often play.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Example opening against 1000 Elo Transformer ModelThat same pattern continued throughout the game. The model regularly overlooked hanging pieces, made questionable exchanges, and occasionally sacrificed major pieces for very little compensation. In other words, it was making the kinds of mistakes that felt recognizably human for a player around the 1000 Elo level.
Playing against the 2000 Elo model felt like facing a completely different opponent. Its openings followed well-established theory, it coordinated its pieces naturally, and it rarely made obvious tactical mistakes.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Opening against 2000 Elo Transformer modelPress enter or click to view image in full size

Middlegame against 2000 Elo Transformer ModelAs the game progressed, the difference became even more apparent. In the middlegame below, the model consistently chose moves that agreed with Stockfish’s top recommendations, maintaining an approximately equal position against me (rated around 1800 Elo). Although it wasn’t perfect, it demonstrated the kind of positional understanding and tactical awareness I’d expect from a strong club player.
Without ever explicitly telling the models how different rating levels should play, they learned distinct playing styles simply by observing human games.
Where It Fell Short
The project also exposed an interesting limitation.
Suppose your opponent blunders a rook in the opening.
A traditional engine immediately captures it.
My 2000 Elo model didn’t.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Player hanging a Rook on their second move. The 2000 Elo AI misses the hanging Rook due to not having training data for this position and no explicit position evaluation.Instead, it continued making natural developing moves.
At first this looked like a failure.
Then I remembered something important.
Nobody around 2000 Elo hangs a rook on move 2.
Because the model was trained to imitate human play rather than search for the objectively best move, and positions in its training set containing such obvious tactical blunders were extremely rare, it continued with natural opening development instead of exploiting the free rook.
The same issue appeared in simplified endgames.
Since the model never learned an explicit positional evaluation function, it sometimes struggled to convert winning positions into checkmate despite making individually reasonable moves.
What I’d Do Next
There are several directions I’d like to explore.
The most obvious is training additional models at intermediate ratings such as 1200, 1400, 1600, and 1800 Elo to see whether the subtle differences between neighboring skill levels also emerge naturally.
Another possibility is combining human move prediction with engine evaluations.
Instead of learning only what humans do, the model could also learn whether those moves improve or worsen the position.
That could preserve the natural feel of human play while helping the model avoid unusual tactical oversights and improve its endgame technique.
Final Thoughts
This project started with a simple question:
Can an AI imitate how humans think about chess instead of simply playing weaker chess?
The answer appears to be yes.
By training transformer models directly on human games, I was able to produce two models that not only differed in playing strength, but also displayed noticeably different styles of decision-making.
More broadly, I think this project highlights something that extends well beyond chess. Machine learning isn’t always about finding the optimal answer. Sometimes the more interesting challenge is understanding how humans actually make decisions, with all of our strengths, weaknesses, and imperfections.
As we continue pushing toward increasingly capable AI systems, it’s worth asking whether optimizing for perfect decisions should always be the goal. Human intelligence isn’t defined solely by optimality. It’s shaped by intuition, creativity, experience, and even the mistakes we make. If we want AI to work alongside us one day, understanding those human qualities may prove even more important than surpassing them.
If you’d like to dive deeper into the methodology, dataset construction, model architecture, and evaluation, I’ve also published the full technical report.
